quiz.title: High-School Geometry: Unit 3 Quiz 4
quiz.subtitle: The Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem
quiz.subject: Geometry
quiz.topic: Geometry: 3.4 Parallel Lines and Same-Side Interior Angles

questions.question: The Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem states that if two parallel lines are cut by a transversal, then same-side interior angles are:
questions.explanation: The Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem states that if two parallel lines are cut by a transversal, then same-side interior angles are supplementary — their measures sum to $180^\circ$.

questions.question: Two parallel lines are cut by a transversal. $\angle 3$ and $\angle 5$ are same-side interior angles. If $m\angle 3 = 72^\circ$, what is $m\angle 5$?
questions.explanation: By the Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem, same-side interior angles are supplementary. So $m\angle 5 = 180^\circ - 72^\circ = 108^\circ$.

questions.question: Two parallel lines are cut by a transversal. $\angle 4$ and $\angle 6$ are same-side interior angles. If $m\angle 4 = 115^\circ$, what is $m\angle 6$?
questions.explanation: Same-side interior angles are supplementary when lines are parallel. So $m\angle 6 = 180^\circ - 115^\circ = 65^\circ$.

questions.question: Two parallel lines are cut by a transversal. $\angle 3 = (2x + 10)^\circ$ and its same-side interior angle $\angle 5 = (3x + 5)^\circ$. What is the value of $x$?
questions.explanation: Same-side interior angles are supplementary: $(2x + 10) + (3x + 5) = 180$. Simplifying: $5x + 15 = 180$, so $5x = 165$, giving $x = 33$.

questions.question: Two parallel lines are cut by a transversal. $\angle 4 = (5x - 8)^\circ$ and its same-side interior angle $\angle 6 = (7x - 4)^\circ$. What is $m\angle 4$?
questions.explanation: Same-side interior angles are supplementary: $(5x - 8) + (7x - 4) = 180$. Simplifying: $12x - 12 = 180$, so $12x = 192$, giving $x = 16$. Then $m\angle 4 = 5(16) - 8 = 72^\circ$.

questions.question: Two lines are cut by a transversal, and same-side interior angles are supplementary. What conclusion can be drawn?
options: The transversal is perpendicular to both lines
questions.explanation: The converse of the Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem states that if same-side interior angles are supplementary, then the lines cut by the transversal are parallel.

questions.question: Two lines are cut by a transversal. $\angle 3 = 95^\circ$ and its same-side interior angle $\angle 5 = 85^\circ$. What must be true?
options: The angles form a linear pair
questions.explanation: Same-side interior angles sum to $95^\circ + 85^\circ = 180^\circ$, so they are supplementary. By the converse of the Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem, the lines are parallel.

questions.question: Two lines are cut by a transversal. $\angle 4 = 60^\circ$ and its same-side interior angle $\angle 6 = 60^\circ$. Are the lines parallel?
options: Yes, because same-side interior angles must be congruent under this standard model setup
Yes, because the two interior angle values sum up to exactly $120^\circ$ across the drawing
No, because same-side interior angles must be supplementary for the lines to be parallel
No, because the intersecting transversal path must be perpendicular to at least one line
questions.explanation: For the lines to be parallel by the converse of the Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem, same-side interior angles must be supplementary (sum to $180^\circ$). Here, $60^\circ + 60^\circ = 120^\circ \neq 180^\circ$, so the lines are not parallel.

questions.question: Two parallel lines are cut by a transversal. $m\angle 3 = 121^\circ$. What is $m\angle 5$ if they are same-side interior angles?
questions.explanation: Same-side interior angles are supplementary when lines are parallel. So $m\angle 5 = 180^\circ - 121^\circ = 59^\circ$.

questions.question: Which statement correctly distinguishes same-side interior angles from alternate interior angles when lines are parallel?
options: Same-side interior angles are congruent; alternate interior angles are supplementary
Same-side interior angles are supplementary; alternate interior angles are congruent
questions.explanation: When lines are parallel, same-side interior angles are supplementary (sum to $180^\circ$), while alternate interior angles are congruent (equal in measure). Both pairs lie between the two lines but differ in their relationship.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered the Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem and its converse, and can distinguish it from the Alternate Interior Angles Theorem.
scoreMessages.70: Good work! You understand the theorem but be careful not to confuse same-side interior angles (supplementary) with alternate interior angles (congruent).
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Focus on: Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem (parallel lines $\rightarrow$ supplementary same-side interior angles) and its converse (supplementary same-side interior angles $\rightarrow$ parallel lines).
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! Same-side interior angles lie between the two lines and on the same side of the transversal. When lines are parallel, they sum to $180^\circ$. When they sum to $180^\circ$, the lines are parallel.
